masters.notes: Systematized monastic rules, established Shi surname convention, created first catalogue of Chinese Buddhist scriptures.
Directed one of the most prolific translation bureaus in Buddhist history. His translations shaped the vocabulary of East Asian Buddhism.
Author of the Zhao Lun, the first mature synthesis of Madhyamaka thought in Chinese.
Founder of Donglin Temple on Mount Lu. First patriarch of Chinese Pure Land Buddhism.
Argued all beings possess buddha-nature. Championed sudden enlightenment, anticipating Chan.

Central Indian monk, one of the most prolific translators of the Liu Song dynasty. Translated the Laṅkāvatāra Sūtra (443 CE), Saṃyuktāgama (50 fascicles), and 50+ other texts. The Lengqie shizi ji (c. 720) retroactively placed him as first patriarch of a Laṅkāvatāra-based Chan lineage transmitting to Bodhidharma, though this connection is historically dubious. Active in Jiankang (Nanjing) under imperial patronage.

First Patriarch of Chan and traditionally the 28th Indian patriarch. Born as a prince in southern India, possibly of the Pallava dynasty. Arrived in China c. 520, reportedly met Emperor Wu of Liang who asked about the merit of his works; Bodhidharma replied 'no merit.' Settled at Shaolin Temple on Mount Song, where tradition says he sat in wall-gazing meditation (壁觀) for nine years. His teaching emphasized direct transmission outside scriptures. Named Huike his dharma heir after Huike demonstrated wordless understanding.

Celebrated lay Buddhist. Credited with inventing the revolving sutra cabinet. Awakened by an obscure foreign monk known as Song Toutuo, sometimes later conflated with Bodhidharma. Revered alongside Bodhidharma and Baozhi as the Three Mahasattvas of the Liang Dynasty.
Second Patriarch. Famous for cutting off his arm to demonstrate sincerity to Bodhidharma.
Nun and direct disciple of the Second Patriarch Huike. One of few women in early Chan lineage histories.
Third Patriarch. Attributed author of the Xinxinming (信心銘, Faith in Mind), one of the earliest and most influential Chan poems.
Traditionally credited as the first to bring Chan teachings to Korea from the Fourth Patriarch Daoxin. Semi-legendary but foundational for Korean Seon.
Fourth Patriarch. First to establish a settled monastic community for Chan practice at Mount Shuangfeng.
Founder of the Niutou (Oxhead) school. Studied under the Fourth Patriarch Daoxin but developed a distinct teaching emphasizing emptiness.
Fifth Patriarch. Presided over the famous poetry contest between Shenxiu and Huineng at East Mountain (Dongshan).

THE foundational Korean Buddhist thinker. Over 80 works. Developed tong bulgyo (interpenetrated Buddhism). Not a Seon master but his Essence-Function framework profoundly shaped Korean Seon character.
Nun mentioned in the Platform Sutra who could recite the Mahāparinirvāṇa Sūtra. Huineng reportedly explained its meaning to her.
Student of the Fifth Patriarch Hongren. Settled in Zizhou, Sichuan. Empress Wu Zetian gave him a patriarchal robe. Died in seated meditation at age 94. Transmitted to Chuji.
Foremost disciple of the Fifth Patriarch in the traditional account. Leader of the Northern school emphasizing gradual cultivation.

Sixth Patriarch and pivotal figure in Chan history. Born 638 in Xinzhou, Guangdong, to a poor family. Received secret transmission from Hongren after composing a verse demonstrating sudden awakening. His Platform Sutra (六祖壇經) established the 'sudden awakening' teaching. All surviving Chan/Zen lineages descend through his five principal dharma heirs. His mummified body is preserved at Nanhua Temple.
Author of the Song of Enlightenment (證道歌). Said to have attained awakening in a single night's meeting with Huineng.
Lay Huayan scholar. His experiential reading of the Avatamsaka profoundly influenced later Chan.
Received patriarchal robe from Zhishen in 702. Key link in the Sichuan Chan lineage recorded in the Lidai fabao ji.
Dharma heir of the Sixth Patriarch. Ancestor of the Caodong, Yunmen, and Fayan lineages.
Listed in the Platform Sutra as one of Huineng's disciples.
Dharma heir of Huineng. Ancestor of the Linji and Guiyang houses. Famous for 'polishing a tile' exchange with Mazu.
Championed Huineng as true Sixth Patriarch against the Northern school. His polemical efforts established Southern school dominance.
Korean-born monk (Kim Heshang) who established a major Chan center in Sichuan. Known for 'three phrases' teaching.
Founded the radical Baotang school in Sichuan, rejecting all external forms. Teachings recorded in the Lidai fabao ji.
Appointed National Teacher by two Tang emperors. Known for 'the National Teacher calls three times' koan.

Early Korean Seon figure (704-779). First studied under Beomnang at Mt. Hogeo, then traveled to Tang China and received transmission from Chigong (a Korean disciple of Puji of the Northern School). Bridges the Beomnang lineage with the Northern School tradition. Attested in the Jijeungdaesa stele (893 CE, composed by Choe Chiwon).

One of the most influential Tang dynasty Chan masters. Born 709 in Sichuan. Founded the Hongzhou school, producing 139 dharma heirs. Known for 'this mind is Buddha' (即心是佛) and 'ordinary mind is the Way' (平常心是道). Pioneer of unconventional teaching methods including shouts and blows.
Ancestor of the Caodong (Soto) lineage. Author of the Cantongqi (參同契, Harmony of Difference and Sameness). Along with Mazu, one of the two great branches of Tang Chan.
Known for 'The mind-moon is solitary and round; its light swallows the ten thousand forms.'
Student Longtan transmitted line that produced Deshan Xuanjian.
Most celebrated lay Chan practitioner. Dumped his wealth into a river and lived making bamboo utensils.
Daughter of Layman Pang, depicted as matching or surpassing her father in insight. Died moments before him.
Second generation Heze lineage. Dharma heir of Shenhui.

Established the first independent Chan monastic code (百丈清規), revolutionizing Chan communal life. Famous for 'a day without work is a day without food' (一日不作一日不食). Subject of the wild fox koan. His monastic regulations became the model for all later Chan/Zen monasteries.
One of Mazu's three great disciples alongside Baizhang and Nanquan.
Prominent student of Mazu who served as abbot of Zhangjing monastery in Chang'an.
Hongzhou school master at Xingshan Temple in Chang’an. Teacher of the poet Bai Juyi, who wrote his memorial inscription in 819.
Dharma heir of Shitou Xiqian. Named after Mount Wuxie where he resided.
Author of the Essentials of Sudden Enlightenment. Mazu called him the 'Great Pearl'.
Third generation Heze lineage. Transmitted to Suizhou Daoyuan.
Obscure link figure in the Beomnang-Daoxin lineage. Known only from the Jijeungdaesa stele at Bongamsa (893 CE, by Choe Chiwon). Transmitted the dharma from Sinhaeng to Hyeeun.
Known for his teaching that the three poisons are themselves the three disciplines.
Famous for burning a wooden Buddha statue to warm himself. Studied with both Shitou and Mazu.

First Korean monk to transmit the Seon (Chan) lineage to the Korean peninsula, inaugurating the 'Nine Mountains' Seon movement. He traveled to Tang China and studied under Xitang Zhizang, a direct heir of Mazu Daoyi, receiving formal dharma transmission. Upon returning to Silla, he found the doctrinal Buddhist establishment hostile to Seon and was unable to teach openly during his lifetime. His disciple Yomgo later founded the Gaji Mountain school (Silsangsa), the first of the Nine Mountains Seon lineages. Doui is thus honored as the patriarch who planted Seon in Korean soil, even though its flowering came after his death. Attested in Zutangji (952): records his study under Xitang Zhizang with a transmission dialogue confirming his awakening.
Pivotal figure linking Shitou's lineage to the later Caodong school through his student Yunyan.
Appears in several encounter dialogues including a well-known exchange with Nanquan.
Fourth generation Heze lineage. Teacher of Guifeng Zongmi.

Early Silla monk who studied under Xitang Zhizang in Tang China and returned to establish the Silsangsan school, one of the Nine Mountain Schools of Korean Seon. His transmission is attested in Korean stele inscriptions and the Zutangji.

One of Mazu's 'three great disciples'. Often called 'Old Teacher Wang' (王老師). After enlightenment under Mazu, retreated to Mount Nanquan for 30 years. Famous for killing the cat (南泉斬貓) and 'ordinary mind is the Way' (平常心是道). Had seventeen dharma heirs.
Featured in the 'condolence call' koan (Blue Cliff Record, Case 55).
Awakened by Mazu's 'This very mind is Buddha.' Became a recluse on Damei mountain.
Resided at Guizong monastery on Mount Lu. Known for dramatic teaching methods.
Teacher of Deshan. Their encounter with the blown-out candle is one of Chan's most celebrated awakening stories.
Dharma heir of Damei Fachang in the Mazu lineage. Teacher of Juzhi, to whom he transmitted the one-finger Chan teaching.
Fifth patriarch of Huayan and Heze Chan lineage holder. His Chan Prolegomenon is a landmark work classifying Chan schools.
Crucial link in the Caodong lineage as teacher of Dongshan. 'Whole body is hands and eyes' koan.
Known for the 'rhinoceros fan' koan (Blue Cliff Record, Case 91).
One of the greatest Tang poets and devoted lay Buddhist. Famous exchange with Bird's Nest Daolin.

Teacher of Linji and one of the most important Tang Chan masters. Born in Fuzhou, Fujian. Teaching centered on 'one mind' (一心): all beings possess this one mind which is the Buddha. His teachings recorded by lay student Pei Xiu in the Chuanxin Fayao (傳心法要) and Wanling Lu (宛陵錄). Named thirteen successors.
Doheon's direct Seon teacher. Known only from the Jijeungdaesa stele at Bongamsa (893 CE, by Choe Chiwon). Transmitted the Beomnang-Daoxin lineage to Doheon, founder of the Huiyangsan school.
Co-founder of the Guiyang school, earliest of the Five Houses.
Formidable laywoman nicknamed 'Iron Grindstone' for her relentless debating style. One of few women in the lamp records.
The 'Boat Monk' who spent decades ferrying passengers. After transmitting to Jiashan, he capsized his boat and drowned.
Famous for answering every question by raising one finger.
Dharma heir of Danxia Tianran.
Known for sitting facing the wall when visitors came.
Dharma heir of Baizhang.
Known for dancing with the rice bucket — 'Bodhisattvas, come eat!'
Reclusive monk in Gao’an, Jiangxi. Triggered Linji Yixuan’s great awakening when Huangbo sent Linji to him. Teacher of Moshan Liaoran.

Silla monk who traveled to Tang China and received dharma transmission from Xitang Zhizang (Mazu lineage). Returned to found the Dongni-san school, one of the Nine Mountain Schools. Teacher of Doseon.
Originally a Diamond Sutra scholar. Famous for 'Thirty blows whether you can speak or not.'

Founder of the Linji (Rinzai) school, one of only two Chan lineages surviving to the present. Asked Huangbo three times about the essence of Buddhism and was beaten each time; enlightened after visiting Gaoan Dayu. Known for radical pedagogy: shouts (喝), blows, and paradoxical statements. Developed 'four shouts' and 'four positions of host and guest' frameworks. His recorded sayings (臨濟錄) compiled by Sansheng Huiran are foundational to the koan tradition.
Known for 'The entire ten-direction world is the eye of the monk.'

Silla monk who studied under Nanquan Puyuan in Tang China. Founded the Sajasan school, one of the Nine Mountain Schools of Korean Seon. His transmission from Nanquan places him in the Mazu Daoyi lineage.

Co-founder of the Caodong (Soto) school. Born 807 in Kuaiji (Shaoxing, Zhejiang). Created the Five Ranks (五位偏正) teaching and the Jewel Mirror Samadhi (寶鏡三昧). Enlightened upon seeing his reflection crossing a stream. Had 26 dharma heirs. Transmitted to Japan by Dogen as the Soto school.

Silla monk who received transmission from Zhangjing Huaihui in Tang China. Founded the Bongnimsan school, one of the Nine Mountain Schools of Korean Seon. His lineage traces through the Mazu Daoyi tradition.
Slammed the gate on Yunmen's leg, triggering his enlightenment. Called 'Chen the Sandal-Weaver.'
Known for the 'watch the dog!' koan.

Silla monk who received transmission domestically from Yeomgeo (a student of Doui), continuing the Gajisan school lineage. Did not travel to China. Attested in Korean stele records and traditional mountain school genealogies.
Awakened when the boatman-monk pushed him into water.

Silla monk associated with the Huiyang-san school, one of the Nine Mountain Schools. Unlike most Nine Mountain founders, he did not travel to Tang China for transmission, making his lineage claim domestically attested only.
Co-founder of the Guiyang school. Known for circular figures in teaching.
Let out a great shout at moment of death during Huang Chao rebellion.
His assembly called 'the dead tree hall' — monks sat in continuous meditation.
Critical link in the survival of the Linji school through the Five Dynasties.
Disciple of Linji Yixuan at Baoshou Monastery in Zhenzhou. Known for exchanges with Zhaozhou and Sansheng.

Silla monk of royal Jinhan lineage who studied under Mayu Baoche in Tang China. Founded the Seongju-san school, one of the Nine Mountain Schools. One of the longest-lived of the Nine Mountain founders.

Founder of the Sagulsan (Mount Sagul) school, one of the Nine Mountains Seon lineages of Korea. He traveled to Tang China and trained under Yanguan Qi'an, a direct disciple of Mazu Daoyi, practicing for six years before receiving dharma transmission. Returning to Silla, he established Gulsan Monastery as his base and built one of the most active Seon communities of ninth-century Korea. His lineage maintained strong ties with the Mazu school's emphasis on everyday-mind-is-the-Way. He received lavish royal patronage, and his stele inscription is an important source for early Korean Seon. Attested in Zutangji (952): records his awakening under Yanguan Qi'an after six years of practice.
Woman Chan master who defeated seventeen traveling monks in dharma combat.
Korean monk with the second-longest entry in the Zutangji (952). Studied under Yangshan Huiji of the Guiyang school. Uniquely synthesized Chan with Huayan doctrine.
Woman Chan master who led her own monastery. Guanxi Zhixian bowed and served as her gardener for three years.

Dharma heir of Dongshan. Known for 'three diseases and two lights of the dharmakaya' teaching. Yunmen served under him before succeeding Xuefeng. Appears in Gateless Gate Case 48 and Book of Serenity Case 61.

One of the most celebrated Chan masters, appearing in more koans than any other figure. Met Nanquan at age 18, studied under him over 20 years. At 80, settled at the dilapidated Guanyin Temple in Zhaozhou where he taught for 40 more years. Famous for 'No' (無), 'wash your bowl', 'the cypress tree in the garden', and 'have a cup of tea' (喫茶去). Lived to 120. His lineage died out by c. 1000 CE, but his recorded sayings (趙州錄) preserved his enormous influence.
Awakened by sound of a pebble striking bamboo after burning his notes.
Initially Linji's student, later studied under Jiashan of the Shitou lineage.

Korean Seon monk and master geomancer (pungsu) who profoundly shaped the political geography of Korea. Trained in both Seon meditation and Chinese geomantic arts (fengshui), he applied topographical analysis to temple and capital siting. His prophecies regarding the decline of Silla and the rise of a new dynasty at Songak (modern Kaesong) were later credited with guiding Wang Geon's founding of the Goryeo dynasty (918). He founded or restored numerous temples based on geomantic principles. Though his geomantic legacy sometimes overshadows his Buddhist teaching, he was recognized as a legitimate Seon master. Attested in Goryeo-era hagiographies: credited with prophecies that legitimized the Goryeo founding, with his geomantic writings preserved in the Doseon bigi.
Compiler/editor of the Linji Lu (Record of Linji). Known for sharp exchanges.
Known for 'one arrow piercing three barriers' koan.
Co-founder of the Caodong (Soto) school. Systematized Dongshan's Five Ranks teaching.
Principal dharma heir of Dongshan. Major monastery on Mount Yunju.
Dharma heir of Shishuang Qingzhu (807-888). Dates corrected from d.950 to d.903 per lineage chronology.
Community of 1500+ monks. His students founded the Yunmen and Fayan houses.
Originally a fisherman. Ancestral figure of the Fayan school. 'Three diseases' teaching.
Known for 'the price of the dharma body' exchange.
Lived as hermit on Mount Touzi for thirty years.
Called out to himself daily 'Master! Be alert! Don't be deceived!'
Known for the 'bowl' koan.
Dharma heir of Yunju Daoying alongside Ieom. One of the Four Fearless Masters of Haedong. Stele preserved in CBETA B31n0170.
Traveled widely before settling under Dongshan.
After Shishuang's death, challenged the assembly about understanding the late master.
Close dharma brother of Changqing Huileng.
Direct ancestor of the Fayan school. 'What is this?' about a rock triggered Fayan's awakening.
Critical link in Linji lineage between Xinghua and Fengxue.
'Cuiyan's eyebrows' koan — tested whether his teaching had been in error.
Caodong lineage patriarch at Tongan Monastery near Nanchang. Successor of Yunju Daoying.
Wore out seven sitting cushions before awakening.

Late Silla / early Goryeo monk who studied under Yunju Daoying (Dongshan Liangjie lineage) in Tang China. Founded the Sumi-san school, the last of the Nine Mountain Schools. Attested primarily in Korean stele inscriptions.
Known for the 'what sound is that?' (raindrops) koan.
Important figure in transmitting the Guiyang school.
Abbot of Gushan (Drum Mountain) monastery in Fuzhou.

Founder of the Yunmen school. Born 864 in Jiaxing. Enlightened when Muzhou slammed the gate on his leg. Established Yunmen Monastery on Yunmen Mountain in Guangdong with 500+ monks. Known for terse, one-word answers: 'A dried shit-stick.' Famous for 'every day is a good day.' His mummified body was preserved.
'If you have a staff, I will give you a staff; if not, I will take it away.'
Founder of the Fayan school, last of the Five Houses. Emphasized integration of doctrinal study with Chan.
Teacher of Xuedou Chongxian, key link in Yunmen lineage.
Yunmen lineage monk who served as abbot of Wuzu Temple (Fifth Patriarch Mountain) in Huangmei. Famous for the legend, constructed by Huihong, that he was reborn as the poet Su Shi (Su Dongpo).

links.label: Duke UP (Past Lives of Su Shi)

masters.notes: Direct heir of Yunmen. Known for his three turning phrases.
Caodong lineage patriarch. Transmitted the Caodong dharma from Tongan Daopi to Liangshan Yuanguan.
Dharma heir of Fayan Wenyi. Resided at Qingliang monastery in Jinling. Famous for the tiger bell koan. Dates corrected: previously d.938 predates teacher Fayan (d.958).
Most important heir of Fayan Wenyi. National teacher of Wuyue kingdom.
Preserved the Linji lineage during Five Dynasties persecutions.

Third patriarch of the Fayan school and author of the Zongjing Lu (宗鏡錄, Record of the Mirror of the School) in 100 fascicles, one of the most comprehensive Chan works ever composed. Student of Tiantai Deshao. Served as abbot of Yongming Monastery in Hangzhou.
Served Yunmen for eighteen years before transmission.
Known for the 'three pounds of hemp' koan.
Preserved the Linji transmission through the difficult Five Dynasties period.
Linji master. Student of Shoushan Xingnian. Teacher of Fushan Fayuan, through whom the Caodong lineage was preserved.
Caodong lineage patriarch. Teacher of Dayang Jingxuan, the last living Caodong lineage holder.
Reviver of the Linji school. Pioneered verse commentary on old cases (songgu).
Last living Caodong lineage holder. Entrusted lineage to Linji master Fushan Fayuan to preserve the school.
His two greatest students founded the Huanglong and Yangqi branches.
Founded the Yangqi branch, which became the dominant Chan lineage.
His poetic verses on 100 cases became the foundation of the Blue Cliff Record. Greatest poet of the Chan tradition.
Dharma brother of Shishuang Chuyuan.
Yunmen school master at Kaixian Temple on Mount Lushan. Teacher of Foyin Liaoyuan.
Received Linji transmission from Shexian Guisheng. Accepted Dayang Jingxuan’s Caodong materials and passed them to Touzi Yiqing, saving the Caodong school from extinction.
Founded the Huanglong branch. Famous for his 'Three Barriers' test.
Key transmitter in the Yangqi branch.
Received Caodong transmission via proxy through Fushan Fayuan. Saved the Caodong lineage from extinction.
Huanglong branch master at Donglin Temple on Mount Lu. Teacher of the poet Su Shi.
Huanglong branch master. Teacher of lay student Zhang Shangying, the Song chancellor.
Famous for his close friendship with the poet Su Shi.
Taught Huang Tingjian using the scent of osmanthus.
One of China's greatest poets. Deeply engaged with Chan Buddhism. Legend connected him to a rebirth of Wuzu Shijie.
Also known as Baofeng Kewen. Second-generation Huanglong master. Known for expelling monks immediately after their awakening. Teacher of Doushuai Congyue.
His three principal disciples became the most influential Chan teachers of the next generation.
Fellow student of Yuanwu Keqin under Wuzu Fayan. His sub-lineage led to Wumen Huikai.
Huanglong branch master. Enlightened by a thunderclap after working on Huitang’s fist koan. Teacher of Kongshi Daoren.

Direct disciple of Furong Daokai. His line carried Caodong transmission northward into Jin territory, eventually reaching Wansong Xingxiu via four generations — a separate branch from the southern Danxia Zichun line.
Teacher of Hongzhi Zhengjue. Key figure in the Caodong revival.
Principal reviver of the Caodong school. Refused an imperial purple robe, preferring imprisonment.
Dharma brother of Yuanwu Keqin. Subtle, penetrating teaching style.
High-ranking Song official who became devoted Chan patron. Author of Hufa lun.
Known for literary skill and poetic dharma talks.
Compiler of the Blue Cliff Record, the most celebrated koan collection in Chan.
His lineage transmitted to Japan as the dominant Rinzai line.
Dharma heir of Lumen Zijue. Continued Furong Daokai's Caodong line in the Jin-controlled north while the southern branch (Danxia Zichun to Hongzhi) flourished under the Song.
Song dynasty Chan master at Langye Mountain. Teacher of Yu Daopo, his only dharma heir.
Helped revive Caodong in the Southern Song. Abbot of Tiantong monastery.
Yangqi branch master. Link between Kaifu Daoning and Laona Zuzheng in the chain to Wumen Huikai.
Leading Caodong master. Advocate of silent illumination (mozhao) Chan. Compiled the Book of Equanimity.
Initially studied Yunmen, received Caodong transmission from Zhenxie Qingliao. Abbot of Tiantong monastery. Teacher of Xuedou Zhijian.
Systematized kanhua (keyword investigation) Chan. Most influential Chan master of the Southern Song.
Key link in the Huqiu lineage.
One of the most prominent female Chan masters. Known for her sharp repartee.
Laywoman who achieved awakening. Notable female figure in Song Chan.
Female Chan master of the Southern Song.
Caodong master of the post-Furong northern lineage. Active in Hebei region during the Jin–Song partition.
Also known as Dahong Zuzheng. Song dynasty Yangqi-line master. Teacher of Yuelin Shiguan.
His most prominent heir Songyuan Chongyue continued the dominant Linji line through Po'an Zuxian and Yunan Puyan.
Caodong master at Mount Xuedou. Teacher of Tiantong Rujing, who transmitted Caodong to Japan through Dogen.
Female Chan practitioner of the Southern Song.
Caodong master; teacher of Xueyan Huiman. Appears in standard Caodong lineage charts between Lingyan Sengbao and Xueyan Huiman. Specific dates not preserved.
Caodong Chan master at Daming Temple in Cizhou. Direct teacher of Wansong Xingxiu; granted him his definitive awakening and dharma transmission.
Founded the Songyuan sub-lineage. His two main dharma heirs Po'an Zuxian and Yunan Puyan produced the lineages of Wuzhun Shifan and Xutang Zhiyu respectively.

Founder of the Jogye Order, the dominant school of Korean Buddhism to the present day. Uniquely among Chan/Seon masters, Jinul achieved three distinct awakenings entirely through reading texts rather than through face-to-face transmission: first from the Platform Sutra, then from Li Tongxuan's commentary on the Avatamsaka Sutra, and finally from Dahui Zonggao's letters on hwadu (huatou) practice. He synthesized 'sudden awakening followed by gradual cultivation' (dono jeomsu) as the normative Seon path, harmonizing Seon meditation with Hwaom (Huayan) doctrinal study. He revitalized Korean Buddhism from his base at Songgwangsa, which he rebuilt as a 'community of the Samgha jewel.' Attested in Buswell (1991), The Korean Approach to Zen: the definitive English-language study of his thought and legacy.
Dharma heir of Songyuan Chongyue. Teacher of Wuzhun Shifan, through whom his lineage produced Xueyan Zuqin and Gaofeng Yuanmiao.
Yangqi branch Linji master. Teacher of Wumen Huikai, compiler of the Gateless Barrier. Gave Wumen the koan of Zhaozhou’s ‘No’.
Dharma heir of Songyuan Chongyue. Teacher of Xutang Zhiyu, whose recorded sayings became profoundly influential in Japanese Rinzai Zen.
Abbot of Tiantong monastery. Transmitted Caodong to Japan through Dogen. Emphasized shikantaza (just sitting).

Jinul's foremost disciple and second master of Songgwangsa. He compiled a collection of 1,125 kongan (koans) titled the Seonmun yeomsong seolhwa (Seon Verses and Commentaries), the first indigenous Korean koan anthology. This monumental work, completed in thirty fascicles, selected cases from Chinese Chan collections and added Korean commentary, establishing an independent Korean Seon literary tradition distinct from mere importation of Chinese texts. He also expanded Songgwangsa's monastic community and received the posthumous title 'National Teacher' (guksa). His koan collection remained central to Korean Seon training for centuries. Attested in Korean Buddhist historical records: the Seonmun yeomsong seolhwa is the first indigenous Korean koan collection, predating comparable Japanese efforts.

Khitan statesman, chief advisor to Genghis Khan and Ögedei Khan; architect of early Mongol civil administration in North China. Lay disciple of Wansong Xingxiu from 1215, dharma name Zhanran Jushi ('Layman Clear-and-Still'). Commissioned Wansong to reconstruct the Book of Serenity in 1224 — without him, Hongzhi Zhengjue's 100 verses would have been lost.

Influential Caodong master active during the late Jin dynasty and early Mongol period. Compiler of the Book of Serenity (從容錄, Congrong Lu, 1224), his prose commentary on Hongzhi Zhengjue's 100 verse-comments on old cases, composed at the urging of his lay disciple Yelü Chucai. Self-styled 'Old Man of Ten Thousand Pines' (萬松老人). Abbot of Bao'en Temple in Yanjing from 1197 until his death. His lineage became the dominant Chinese Caodong transmission through his disciple Xueting Fuyu at Shaolin.
One of the most eminent Southern Song Chan masters. Dharma heir of Po'an Zuxian. His portrait is a Japanese national treasure.
3rd patriarch of Suseonsa/Songgwangsa. Led the community for ~18 years after Hyesim's death.

Compiler of the Wumenguan (無門關, Gateless Barrier), the most widely studied koan collection. Attained awakening after six years of working on Zhaozhou's 'No'. The Wumenguan's 48 cases with verse commentary became central to Rinzai training.
Late Song Linji master whose recorded sayings became profoundly influential in Japanese Rinzai Zen. Dharma heir of Yunan Puyan (not Wuzhun Shifan as sometimes misattributed).
4th National Teacher of Suseonsa. Named Royal Teacher (Wangsa) in 1259.

Most historically consequential heir of Wansong. Appointed by Kublai Khan to restore Shaolin Monastery, where he installed the Caodong lineage as the dominant transmission. Composed the 70-character generation poem still used to name Shaolin monks today, institutionalizing the lineage. Through him, Wansong's line became the 'unbroken' Shaolin Caodong transmission surviving to the present.

Primary monastic heir of Wansong Xingxiu. Succeeded him as abbot of Bao'en Temple in Yanjing. Compiled two major koan collections — Konggu ji (Empty Valley Collection) on Touzi Yiqing's verses and Xutang ji (Empty Hall Collection) on Danxia Zichun's verses. Invited to the Mongol court in 1268.
5th National Teacher of Suseonsa. Studied under Hyesim, Mongyeo, and Honwon.
Known for extreme asceticism and fierce teaching style. His autobiography is a classic.

Goryeo-era monk and historian, author of the Samguk yusa (Memorabilia of the Three Kingdoms), one of the most important sources for early Korean history and Buddhism. Associated with the Gajisan school tradition.
6th National Teacher of Suseonsa (originally numbered 7th; numbering corrected per CBETA sources).
Lived on Tianmu mountain in a hermitage. Writings on the huatou (critical phrase) method and 'great doubt' highly influential.
10th National Teacher of Suseonsa. Bridges into the 14th century.

Yuan dynasty Linji master, dharma heir of Xueyan Zuqin and dharma brother of Gaofeng Yuanmiao. Teacher of both Shiwu Qinggong and Pingshan Chulin, making him the indirect ancestor of all late-Goryeo Korean Seon. Active at Xifeng Temple (Jianyang) and Daochang Mountain Wanshou Temple (Huzhou). Attested in DILA Authority Database (A021593).
Most celebrated Yuan-dynasty master. Declined imperial appointments. Strong ties with Korean and Japanese monks.
Yuan dynasty Linji master at Lingyin Temple, Hangzhou. Teacher of Chushi Fanqi.

Yuan dynasty Linji Chan master, dharma heir of Gaofeng Yuanmiao. Significant for Korean Seon history as the Chinese teacher who transmitted to both Taego Bou and Baegun Gyeonghan, linking Korean Seon to mainstream Chinese Linji lineage. Also received teaching from Ji'an Zongxin, a dharma brother of Gaofeng.
Founded Shizi Lin (Lion Grove Garden) in Suzhou. Combined Chan with Pure Land.
Yuan dynasty Linji master. Dharma heir of Zhongfeng Mingben. His line continued through Wanfeng Shiwei to the Ming revival.

Yuan dynasty Linji master, 19th generation (not 18th as sometimes claimed in Korean sources). Dharma brother of Shiwu Qinggong — both studied under Ji'an Zongxin. Gave formal dharma transmission to the Korean monk Naong Hyegeun at Jingci Temple in Hangzhou. Also served as abbot of Lingyin Temple. Authored Jingjuyin (淨居吟), over 200 poems. Attested in DILA Authority Database (A016698).
Major Chan poet of the Yuan-Ming transition.

Korean Seon master best known as the compiler and printer of the Jikji simche yojeol (Anthology of Great Buddhist Priests' Zen Teachings), printed at Heungdeoksa temple in Cheongju in 1377 using movable metal type. This is the oldest known book in the world printed with movable metal type, predating Gutenberg's Bible by 78 years. Baegun traveled to Yuan China and received transmission from Shiwu Qinggong (Stone House), the hermit-poet of Tianmu mountain. His Jikji compiled essential Seon teachings from the patriarchs and was intended as a practical guide for practitioners. Attested in UNESCO Memory of the World Register (2001): the Jikji is recognized as the world's oldest extant movable metal type printed book, with the surviving second volume held by the Bibliotheque nationale de France.

One of the most celebrated Korean Seon masters of the late Goryeo dynasty, remarkable for having received dharma transmission from two masters of entirely different lineages. He first studied under the Indian monk Zhigong (Dhyanabhadra, d. 1363), who had come to Yuan China and gave Naong transmission in an Indian-derived lineage. He then received a second transmission from the Chinese Linji master Pingshan Chulin, a heir in the mainstream Yangqi-Linji line. This double transmission from both Indian and Chinese sources gave Naong unique authority. He became Royal Teacher (wangsa) and initiated major reforms of Korean Seon before his sudden death. Attested in Korean Buddhist records and Chinese Chan genealogies: both transmissions are documented, with his Indian lineage through Zhigong preserved in Goryeo-era inscriptions.
Late Yuan / early Ming Linji master. Key link between Yuan-era Chan and the Ming revival.

The most important institutional figure in Korean Seon history, credited with unifying the Nine Mountains Seon schools into a single Jogye Order. He traveled to Yuan China and received dharma transmission from the hermit-poet Shiwu Qinggong (Stone House) on Tianmu mountain, returning with Shiwu's robe (cassock) and staff as physical proof of transmission. As Royal Teacher (wangsa) under King Gongmin, he reorganized Korean Seon, ending centuries of fragmentation among rival mountain schools. His unification established the institutional framework that persists in Korean Buddhism today. Though Ahn (2019) has questioned the later Joseon-era lineage claims connecting Hyujeong back through Taego, Taego's own Chinese transmission is well documented. Attested in UNDV 2011 conference paper: confirms the Shiwu transmission with physical evidence including the cassock and cane still preserved.
Convergence point where Taego and Naong lineages merge. Also studied under Naong. Critical link to Hyujeong's lineage, though Ahn (2019) notes this chain is a 17th-century construction.

Royal preceptor and chief advisor to Yi Seonggye (King Taejo), the founder of the Joseon dynasty (1392). Muhak's most famous contribution was selecting the site of the new capital at Hanyang (modern Seoul) using geomantic principles, a decision that shaped the political geography of Korea to the present day. As Naong's dharma heir, he carried the unified Seon lineage into the new dynasty and served as a bridge between the Goryeo Buddhist establishment and the Joseon court, even as Neo-Confucian officials increasingly marginalized Buddhism. He designed the layout of key Seoul temples including Hoeryongsa. Attested in Joseon-era court records: his role in the capital selection and his advisory relationship with King Taejo are documented in the Joseon wangjo sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty).

Naong Hyegeun's primary dharma heir and the master who rebuilt Songgwangsa, the 'Sangha Jewel Temple' originally established by Jinul. During the late Goryeo and early Joseon transition, he preserved the Seon monastic community through a period of political upheaval and growing anti-Buddhist sentiment from Neo-Confucian reformers. His reconstruction of Songgwangsa ensured the physical and institutional survival of the Jinul-Hyesim legacy. He maintained the unified Jogye lineage established by Taego Bou, transmitting it into the fifteenth century. Attested in Songgwangsa temple records: his rebuilding efforts and dharma lineage from Naong are preserved in monastery inscriptions and chronicles.
Also known as Hamheo Deuktong. Wrote Hyeonjeong non defending Buddhism against Neo-Confucian attacks. Commentaries on Diamond Sutra and Sutra of Perfect Enlightenment. No known dharma heirs.
Obscure transmission link in the Taego-to-Hyujeong chain. First documented in a 1625 source. Poorly attested.
Early Ming Linji master in the Zhongfeng Mingben line.
Transmission link during the darkest period of Joseon Buddhist suppression. Active when monks were banned from cities.
Mid-Ming Linji master. His heir Wuwen Mingcong continued the lineage.
Shaolin Monastery abbot. Teacher of Xiaoshan. Mid-Ming Caodong transmission through Shaolin.
Maintained Seon transmission during severe Joseon suppression. Name ('Green Pine') suggests mountain hermit life.
Ming dynasty Linji master. Teacher of both Xiaoyan Debao and Yungu Fahui.

Korean Seon monk who achieved the most significant restoration of Buddhist institutional power during the Joseon dynasty's sustained suppression of Buddhism. Under the patronage of Queen Munjeong (regent 1545-1565), he was appointed to the newly restored office of chief monk (panju), reopened the monk examination system, and rebuilt numerous temples. He briefly restored Buddhism to something approaching its former Goryeo-era status. However, his fortunes were entirely tied to the queen: when Munjeong died in 1565, the hostile Neo-Confucian officials immediately stripped him of office, and he was arrested and died under torture or in exile the same year. His rise and fall illustrates the precarious position of Buddhism under Joseon Confucian hegemony. Attested in Joseon wangjo sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty): records both his appointment under Queen Munjeong and his death following her passing.
Shaolin Monastery abbot (1557-1566). Sent warrior monks against Japanese pirates. Key bridge figure connecting Yuan-era Shaolin Caodong to the late Ming revival.
Produced the two greatest 16th-century Seon teachers: Hyujeong (Seosan) and Buhyu Seonsu. All modern Korean Seon traces through him.
Ming dynasty Chan master who pioneered 7-day meditation retreats. Teacher of Hanshan Deqing, one of the Four Great Monks of the Ming.
Ming dynasty Chan elder at Fatong Temple in Beijing. Teacher of Zibo Zhenke for nine years.
Ming dynasty Linji master. Teacher of Yunqi Zhuhong. His teaching featured the stick and thunderous shout.
67th Caodong patriarch. Studied at Shaolin under Xiaoshan, then retreated to Bingshan for 20 years.
One of the 'four great monks' of late Ming. Spearheaded the Jiaxing canon printing project. Died in prison.

The most revered figure in Joseon-era Korean Buddhism, known both as a profound Seon master and as the warrior-monk who organized Buddhist militia (seungbyeong) to resist the Japanese invasion of 1592 (Imjin War). His treatise Seon'ga gwigam (Mirror for Seon Students) became the standard primer for Korean Seon practice, synthesizing Seon, Gyo (doctrinal study), and Yeombul (Pure Land recitation) into a unified curriculum still used today. At age 73, when Toyotomi Hideyoshi's forces invaded, King Seonjo called upon him to rally monks as soldiers; he mobilized thousands across the peninsula. He traced his lineage through Taego Bou to the Chinese Linji school, though Ahn (2019) has challenged the historicity of the intermediate links in this lineage chain. Attested in Jorgensen (2015): provides a full English translation and study of the Seon'ga gwigam.

Hyujeong's foremost disciple, celebrated equally as a Seon master, military commander, and diplomat. During the Imjin War (1592-1598), he led monk-soldiers in combat against the Japanese forces, distinguishing himself at several engagements. After the war, he was sent to Japan as an envoy in 1604 and successfully negotiated the return of approximately 3,500 Korean prisoners of war, a diplomatic feat that made him a national hero. His combination of martial valor and diplomatic skill during Korea's greatest military crisis cemented the image of Buddhist monks as defenders of the nation, counterbalancing the Neo-Confucian narrative that Buddhism was socially useless. Attested in Joseon wangjo sillok and Japanese records: his diplomatic mission to Japan and the repatriation of prisoners are documented in both Korean and Japanese sources.
Late Ming Linji master. Teacher of Miyun Yuanwu, the most influential 17th-century Chan figure.
One of the 'four great monks'. Leading advocate of Chan-Pure Land synthesis.
Dharma brother of Hyujeong/Seosan. Founded the Buhyu branch, one of two major streams of modern Korean Seon.
68th Caodong patriarch. Founded the Shouchang sublineage. Revived work-ethic monastic practice. Promoted Dahui's kanhua Chan within Caodong.
One of the 'four great monks'. Prolific writer, exiled to Guangdong. His autobiography is a classic.
Disciple of Hanshan Deqing. Tonsure master of Ouyi Zhixu at Yunqi Temple.
69th Caodong patriarch. Also called Boshan. Author of Canxue zhijin (Exhortation on Great Doubt), a classic on huatou practice.
Clashed with his teacher over Linji's teaching, splitting the late Ming Linji revival.
Dharma brother of Miyun Yuanwu under Huanyou. Founded a separate Linji branch. Teacher of the imperial Chan line.
Dharma heir of Miyun Yuanwu. Teacher of the eminent nun Qiyuan Xinggang. Used huatou (critical phrase) practice.
Central figure of the late Ming Linji revival. Made his line the dominant Chan transmission into the Qing.

Founded the Pyeonyang-pa, the dominant branch through which ALL modern Jogye Order lineage traces. War lieutenant during the Imjin War (1592-98). More significant for lineage than his dharma brother Yujeong/Samyeong, who was more famous as diplomat.
Third dharma brother of Miyun and Tianyin. Controversially claimed transmission from the long-dead Yunmen Wenyan.
Linji master of the late Ming / early Qing transition.
Last of the 'four great monks'. Primarily Tiantai and Pure Land, fierce advocate of Vinaya reform.
Brought Shouchang Caodong to Fujian. Restored Gushan Yongquan Monastery (1634). Over 100 volumes of writings. Called 'the last successful Caodong Chan master.'
Founded the Caodong Tianjie lineage at Nanjing. Ming loyalist. Innovative synthesis of Chan and Confucian traditions.
Major post-Hyujeong figure. Led reconstruction of Hwaeomsa (1630-1636). Extended the Buhyu lineage into mid-Joseon.
Second abbot of Huangbo monastery after Miyun. Published the controversial Wudeng yantong (1654) asserting Linji orthodoxy. CBETA: J26nB178.
Carried the Pyeonyang lineage into mid-17th century. Important link figure in the main trunk of Korean Seon.
Revived Chan in southwest China (Sichuan). Called 'Little Shakyamuni.' Founded Shuanggui Temple (1653). Had 87 dharma heirs.
Chinese Chan master who emigrated to Japan in 1654. Founded Obaku school. Entirely Chinese-trained. His departure was a loss for Chinese Chan. CBETA: J27nB193.
First to succeed Miyun at Tiantong (1642). Summoned to Beijing as Chan teacher to the Shunzhi Emperor. CBETA: J26nB180, J26nB181.
Imperial Chan teacher to the Shunzhi Emperor. Titled Yulin Guoshi (National Teacher). Dissuaded the emperor from abdicating to become a monk.
Yulin's student. Included in the Yongzheng Emperor's Yuxuan yulu.
Bridged 17th into 18th century. Maintained the Pyeonyang lineage through deepening Joseon suppression.
Link in the imperial Chan line from Yulin to Yongzheng.
Chan teacher of the Yongzheng Emperor before his enthronement. Died on Mount Lu; the emperor issued a special edict honoring his death.

Major 18th-century figure. Authored Seonmun ojonggangyo (Essentials of the Five Schools of Seon), which systematized Korean Seon doctrine. Was exiled after a controversy. The modern Jogye Order's kanhwa Seon tradition traces through him.
Shaolin Monastery Caodong transmission. Link figure between Xueting Fuyu and the late Ming Caodong revival.
Yuan dynasty Shaolin Caodong transmission.
Yuan dynasty Shaolin Caodong transmission.
Yuan-Ming transition. Shaolin Caodong transmission. Dates approximate.
Early Ming Shaolin Caodong transmission.
Early Ming Shaolin Caodong transmission.
Mid Ming Shaolin Caodong transmission. Teacher of Yuezhou Wenzai.

Obscure transmission link in the Pyeonyang lineage between Hwanseong Jian (d.1729) and Cheongbong Geoan (1710-1793). No independent biographical sources survive. Known only from Joseon-era lineage charts. The 68th Korean patriarch in the Jogye reckoning.

Mid-18th century Seon master in the Pyeonyang lineage. One of the few masters in the Hwanseong-to-Gyeongheo chain with attested dates (1710-1793). The 69th Korean patriarch. Carried the lineage through the deepest period of Joseon Neo-Confucian suppression of Buddhism.

Transmission link in the Pyeonyang lineage. The 70th Korean patriarch. No independent biographical sources survive. Known from lineage charts where dharma was transmitted 'from Cheongbong and Geo-ae to Yulbong and Cheonggo.'
Transmission link in the Pyeonyang lineage. The 71st Korean patriarch. No independent biographical sources survive. Known only from Joseon-era lineage charts.

Transmission link in the Pyeonyang lineage. The 72nd Korean patriarch. According to Han Yongun's 'Brief Chronology,' Gyeongheo 'personally received the dharma inheritance from Yongam Seunim,' suggesting Yongam was still alive when Gyeongheo began his training, though the direct teacher-student relationship was with Manhwa Boseon.

Late 18th-early 19th century Seon master. The 73rd Korean patriarch. One of the few masters in the intermediate chain with attested dates (1738-1823). Teacher of Manhwa Boseon, who in turn taught Gyeongheo.

The 74th Korean patriarch. Lecturer on Buddhist doctrine at Donghaksa Monastery on Gyeryongsan Mountain near Daejeon. Took in the teenage Gyeongheo after Venerable Gyeheo left the order, teaching him the doctrinal curriculum. Gyeongheo later told followers to honor Manhwa as the one 'from whom I received the work of the dharma.'

The 75th Korean patriarch and the reviver of modern Korean Seon. Born Song Donguk (宋東旭) in Jeonju. Entered monastic life around age 9 under Venerable Gyeheo at Cheonggyesa, then studied under Manhwa at Donghaksa. Attained enlightenment in 1879 after encountering plague victims, which drove him from sutra study to intensive meditation. On November 15, 1887, achieved great awakening. Revived kanhwa Seon (hwadu meditation) and taught at Beomeosa, Haeinsa, and Songgwangsa. In 1905, at age 59, abruptly left the sangha and wandered incognito in northern Korea, reportedly living as a layperson teaching Confucian classics until his death in 1912 at Gapsan. His four great disciples -- Suwol (1855-1928), Hyewol (1861-1937), Mangong (1871-1946), and Hanam (1876-1951) -- became the pillars of 20th-century Korean Buddhism.

The only Chinese emperor to claim Chan enlightenment. Compiled the Yuxuan yulu (1733), a 19-fascicle anthology of Chan recorded sayings. Authored Jianmo bianyi lu, banning Hanyue Fazang's works and settling the Miyun-Hanyue controversy in Miyun's favor. Lay Buddhist name Yuanming jushi. Reshaped Chan orthodoxy by imperial decree.

Only dharma heir of Yongjue Yuanxian. Established Gushan Yongquan Monastery as a hereditary Caodong dharma-transmission seat. The Gushan Caodong line continued through his successors for 250 years, eventually producing Xuyun.
Based at Longshan Temple in Guangdong. Transmitted Miyun's Linji lineage to the Vietnamese monk Nguyen Thieu, who brought it to Cochinchina in 1665.

Chinese monk from Chaozhou, Guangdong who brought Linji Chan to Vietnam in 1665. Founded Thap Thap Di Da Temple and Quoc An Temple in Hue. 33rd generation Linji. Patronized by the Nguyen lords. His lineage (Lam Te) remains one of the largest in Vietnamese Buddhism.
